title: Daily good conduct (sadvritta) for wellbeing
excerpt: Ayurveda has always known that how you behave shapes how you feel: sadvritta, the code of good daily conduct, is the mental and social hygiene that the classical texts place alongside diet and herbs as a pillar of lasting health.
at_a_glance: Sadvritta is the Ayurvedic code of good daily conduct covering speech, senses, relationships, and inner life, all of which the classical texts treat as health practices.
Suppressing natural urges, speaking harshly, and chronic exposure to hostility are named as direct causes of disease in Caraka Samhita and Ashtanga Hridaya.
Simple daily habits: a two-second pause before reactive speech, quieting sensory inputs before sleep, and a brief evening self-reflection can shift the nervous system over time.

body.heading: when your mood follows your morning
paragraphs: Most of us notice it without naming it: the day you skip breakfast and snap at a colleague, or the evening you scrolled too long and woke up foggy. Ayurveda noticed it too, centuries ago, and gave the pattern a name: sadvritta, often translated as "right conduct" or "code of good behaviour." The word comes from sat (good, virtuous) and vritta (conduct, way of living). But do not let the moral-sounding label put you off. In the classical tradition, sadvritta is not primarily about ethics in a religious sense. It is about the practical hygiene of mind and relationship, the daily habits that keep the nervous system calm, the mind clear, and the body resilient.

Caraka Samhita devotes significant attention to sadvritta in the context of preventive medicine, grouping it alongside dinacharya (daily routine) and ritucharya (seasonal routine) as things a person does to stay healthy rather than to treat illness (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). The logic is straightforward: if mental agitation, social friction, and poor behavioural habits are causes of disease, then their opposites must be causes of health.
body.pullQuote: speak only what is true, kind, and useful

body.heading: what the texts actually say
paragraphs: The guidelines in sadvritta cover four broad areas: how you treat your own body and senses, how you speak, how you relate to others, and how you manage your inner life. They are listed in detail in Caraka Samhita (Sutrasthana and Indriyasthana) and are echoed in Ashtanga Hridaya (Sutrasthana), which consolidates them for everyday use.

On the body and senses: wake before sunrise when the atmosphere is sattvic, or clear and calm. Do not suppress natural urges (hunger, thirst, sleep, elimination) beyond a reasonable point, because habitual suppression of these, known as vegadharan, is listed as a direct cause of disease (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). Eat only when genuinely hungry. Keep your sense organs, eyes, ears, skin, clean and gently exercised.

On speech: speak the truth, but also speak it gently. The phrase satyam priyam hitam, meaning true, pleasing, and beneficial, appears as the standard for words worth saying. Harsh speech, gossip, and lying are explicitly named as causes of mental disturbance in both Caraka Samhita and Ashtanga Hridaya (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). This is not merely a social nicety. Ayurveda understands that every act of speech has a physical correlate: it raises or settles vata, the dosha most linked to anxiety and mental unsteadiness.

On relationships: the texts advise giving respectfully to those in need, associating with people who are calm and knowledgeable, and avoiding those who are chronically angry, envious, or manipulative. Ashtanga Hridaya frames this as protecting ojas, the subtle essence of immunity and vitality. Prolonged exposure to hostility depletes ojas just as surely as a poor diet does (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

On the inner life: the Caraka Samhita places the regulation of desire and anger at the centre of mental health. Desires that are impossible, harmful, or obsessive are to be recognised and set aside, not forcibly suppressed but gently redirected. The same text recommends periodic self-reflection and the company of a teacher or mentor as ways to keep perspective clear (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana).

body.heading: gentle ways to bring sadvritta into an ordinary day
paragraphs: None of this requires a retreat or a radical life change. The beauty of sadvritta is that it is modular: you add one habit at a time and watch whether it settles or disturbs your system.

Start with speech, because it is immediately testable. For one week, pause for two seconds before replying when you feel irritated. Notice whether the irritation passes or grows. The classical advice to speak only what is true, kind, and useful is not idealism. It is a practical check on the mental churn that impulsive words create.

Then look at your sensory diet. What are you reading, watching, and listening to in the hour before sleep? Ashtanga Hridaya (Sutrasthana) is explicit that overstimulation of the senses in the evening disturbs sleep and, over time, digestion. Quiet the inputs and the mind tends to follow.

Make small rituals of respect: greet your parents, teachers, or elders each morning; offer a moment of gratitude before eating. These are not empty ceremonies in the Ayurvedic view. They orient the mind toward receiving and giving rather than taking and reacting, a shift that has measurable effects on stress levels over time.

Finally, spend a few minutes each evening in what modern psychology might call a brief self-audit: where did I speak harshly today, where did I act from greed or fear, and what can I do differently tomorrow? Caraka Samhita calls this kind of reflection a core practice of a wise person (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana).

body.heading: when to see a doctor or vaidya
paragraphs: Sadvritta is preventive in nature and is meant to support, not replace, professional care. If you are experiencing persistent anxiety, depression, mood swings, or intrusive thoughts that interfere with daily life, please consult a qualified psychiatrist, psychologist, or Ayurvedic physician. Seek help promptly if you notice signs such as prolonged inability to sleep, significant changes in appetite or weight, withdrawal from people you care about, or any thoughts of self-harm. A qualified vaidya can assess your prakriti and vikruti and offer a personalised plan that goes well beyond these general guidelines. This article is general health information and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for individualised medical care.

try_this_today.title: The two-second pause
try_this_today.description: When you feel irritated and want to reply immediately, count silently to two first. Ayurveda links impulsive speech to vata aggravation; this single pause reduces mental churn more than any supplement.

try_this_today.title: Quiet the inputs before sleep
try_this_today.description: Switch off screens and loud music at least 30 minutes before bed. Ashtanga Hridaya links evening overstimulation of the senses to disturbed sleep and weakened digestion over time.

try_this_today.title: Greet an elder each morning
try_this_today.description: A conscious act of respect to a parent, teacher, or mentor orients the mind toward giving rather than taking. The texts treat this not as ceremony but as a daily reset of inner attitude.

try_this_today.title: The evening self-audit
try_this_today.description: Spend two to three minutes before sleep asking: where did I speak harshly, where did I act from fear or greed, and what can I do differently tomorrow? Caraka Samhita recommends this kind of reflection for mental clarity.

try_this_today.title: Eat only when genuinely hungry
try_this_today.description: Sadvritta includes respecting the body's signals. Eating from boredom or stress is a form of ignoring natural urges, which the classical texts link to metabolic and emotional imbalance over time.

words_to_know.definition: Right conduct or code of good behaviour: the Ayurvedic guidelines for daily mental, social, and sensory habits that protect health.
Wilful suppression of natural urges (hunger, thirst, sleep, elimination), listed in classical texts as a direct cause of disease.
The subtle essence of vitality and immunity in Ayurveda, said to be depleted by chronic stress, hostility, and poor lifestyle choices.
Clear, calm, and harmonious in quality; the pre-dawn atmosphere is considered sattvic and therefore favourable for waking and practice.
Daily routine: the Ayurvedic schedule of morning to evening practices that keep the body and mind in balance.
Individual constitution: the unique combination of doshas a person is born with, which informs what habits and foods suit them best.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
